title: One-Day Sourdough
text: An actual sourdough — wild culture, no commercial yeast — but brought in within a single day using a store-bought dry sourdough starter. Same method as my semi-sourdough, one swap.

title: One-Day Sourdough
text:
The one that finally tastes like *sourdough* — proper wild-culture tang — but without the three-day wait. The trick is a **store-bought dry sourdough starter** (a powdered culture) in place of yeast: it does both the lift and the flavour, and it's lively enough to bring the whole bake in within a day.

Everything else is my [Semi-Sourdough](/baking/semi-sourdough) method — same folds, same bulk, same cold proof and bake. The only real change is swapping the pinch of yeast for the dry starter, plus this flour blend.

## The dough

- All-purpose flour — 350 g
- Whole wheat flour — 50 g
- Rye flour — 35 g
- **Dry sourdough starter (powder) — 10 g** — no commercial yeast
- Salt — 8 g
- Water — 310–320 g

That's ~435 g flour at roughly **72% hydration**.

## Method

Follow the [Semi-Sourdough](/baking/semi-sourdough) process exactly — mix (water, flour, the dry starter), rest, work in the salt, then the fold rhythm, bulk, shape, cold-proof and bake. The dry starter simply slots in wherever the yeast would have gone.

title: Shokupan (Tangzhong Milk Bread)
text: My go-to soft, cloud-like milk bread — a tangzhong roux keeps it pillowy for days. The base I riff on most.

title: Shokupan (Tangzhong Milk Bread)
text:
These days, milk bread is what I bake most. A cooked-flour paste (tangzhong) pre-gelatinises some of the starch, which lets the dough hold more water — so the crumb stays soft and tearable for days.

I treat this formula as a **base, not a rule**: I'll dial the richness up or down — more or less butter, egg and sugar — depending on how indulgent I want that day's loaf.

## Tangzhong

Whisk together and cook over low heat until it thickens to a paste, then cool:

- Flour — 30 g
- Milk — 220 g

## Dough

- Strong bread flour — 370 g
- Milk powder — 20 g
- Sugar — 40 g
- Instant yeast — 8 g
- Salt — 10 g
- Butter — 60 g
- Milk — 60 g
- 1 egg + 1 yolk
- …plus the cooled tangzhong

## Method

1. Mix everything except the butter into a shaggy dough, then knead until it starts to come together.
2. Add the **butter** and knead to a smooth, strong, windowpane dough.
3. Bulk ferment until roughly doubled.
4. Divide, shape into rolls, and nestle them into a loaf tin (or bake free-form as a pull-apart).
5. Proof until puffy and risen near the rim.
6. Bake at **~180 °C** until deep golden and hollow-sounding, ~25–30 min. Brush with butter while warm for that glossy top.

title: Sweet Bread
text: My best sweet bread to date — a soft, rich enriched loaf, built on the dough I use for cinnamon rolls.

title: Sweet Bread
text:
A soft, rich sweet bread built on the same enriched dough I make for cinnamon rolls. Shaped into tight rounds and baked together, it pulls apart into a pillowy, golden loaf. My best sweet bread so far.

## Dough

- All-purpose flour — 440 g
- Sugar — 60 g (up to 70 g for a sweeter loaf)
- Salt — 6 g
- Yeast — 7 g (1 packet)
- Egg — 1
- Milk — 240 ml (start with ~220 ml, adjust as needed)
- Butter — 50 g, softened

## Method

1. **Mix** — warm milk (not hot) + yeast; add the sugar and egg; add flour + salt and mix to a dough.
2. **Knead** 5–8 min, then add the **butter** slowly and knead until smooth. Aim for a light windowpane.
3. **First rise** — cover and rest 1–1.5 hrs, until doubled.
4. **Shape** — divide into ~60–80 g balls, shape tight and round, and nestle them together in a tin.
5. **Second rise** — 45–60 min, until puffy.
6. **Bake** — 170–175 °C for **28–30 min**. The moment it comes out, **brush with butter or milk and drape a cloth over it for 10 minutes**. That traps the steam and softens the crust into the soft, brittle bakery finish I'm after.

## Shaping for height — the three-dome loaf

The taller, lighter version (the one with the three domes on top in the photos):

- Take a **250 g flour** dough and **divide it into 3 equal pieces**.
- **Roll each piece out thin** with a rolling pin, then roll it up into a tight spiral and set the three side by side in the tin.
- Baked together, they rise into a **taller loaf** with a **lighter crumb** and that bakery-style **three-dome top**.

**The shaping trick that changed everything:** roll each piece *really* thin — aim for **~5–7 mm**. That thinness is what builds the long gluten sheets, so the baked crumb pulls apart into **strands, like shokupan**, instead of a dense crumb. It's the single biggest improvement I've made to the texture.

## Notes

- Keep the dough soft and slightly tacky — don't over-flour.
- Tight, smooth shaping is what gives that bakery pull-apart look.
- **Dial it rustic:** the same dough with less sugar and butter bakes into a deeper-crusted, more rustic loaf (the darker one in the photos) — less rich, just as soft.

title: Semi-Sourdough
text: My everyday loaf — no starter, just a whisper of yeast and a long, slow proof doing the flavour work. A simpler bake while I build up to the full three-day sourdough.

title: Semi-Sourdough
text:
The loaf I actually bake most weeks. **No starter here** — just a **whisper of commercial yeast (~0.5 g for a ~450 g dough)** and a long, slow proof. Given enough cold, patient time, that tiny amount of yeast still builds an open, custardy crumb and a soft tang: the flavour comes from the slow ferment, not a sourdough culture. It's my everyday bread while I work up to the full [three-day sourdough](/baking/sourdough).

## The dough

- Strong bread flour — I work to a ~450 g batch
- **Hydration — 67%, working up to 70%** as your flour and handling allow (≈ 300–315 g water per 450 g flour)
- **Yeast — ~0.5 g per 450 g** — the only leavening; the long, slow proof does the rest
- Salt — added after the mix (~2% of flour)

> Hydration is a feel thing: start at 67% and only push toward 70% once you understand your flour's strength and trust your dough handling.

## Bulk fermentation & folds

The fold rhythm I follow, adapted from [@sourdough_explained](https://www.instagram.com/sourdough_explained/)'s two approaches — pick whichever suits your day:

| Time | Approach 1 | Approach 2 |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 10:00 | Mix dough (water, flour, yeast) | Mix dough (water, flour, yeast) |
| 11:00 | Add salt | Add salt |
| 11:30 | Fold 1 (stretch & fold) | — |
| 12:00 | Fold 2 (coil fold) | Fold 1 (stretch & fold) |
| 12:30 | Fold 3 (coil fold) | — |
| 13:00 | Fold 4 (coil fold) | Fold 2 (coil fold) |
| 14:00 | — | Fold 3 (coil fold) |
| 15:00 | — | Fold 4 (coil fold) |
| 15:30 | Preshape (optional) | Preshape (optional) |
| 16:00 | Shape | Shape |

After shaping, cold-proof, and bake as you would a sourdough.

title: Plantain Doughnuts
text: A soft enriched yeasted dough sweetened with roasted ripe plantain, fried and rolled in sugar.

title: Plantain Doughnuts
text:
Not your classic ring doughnut. A soft, enriched yeasted dough sweetened with **roasted ripe plantain**, fried and rolled in sugar while still warm — softer and sweeter than the usual.

## Ingredients

- 2 ripe yellow plantains, roasted & mashed (~½ cup mash)
- Warm milk — 180 ml (40–41 °C / 105 °F)
- Active dry yeast — 11 g
- Sugar — 55 g
- Eggs — 2
- Unsalted butter — 113 g, melted & cooled
- All-purpose flour — 500 g (start around 450 g, add as needed)
- Salt — 8 g
- Optional: 1 tsp vanilla
- Optional: ¼ tsp nutmeg or cinnamon

## Method

1. Stir the **yeast** into the **warm milk** and let it bloom.
2. Mix in the sugar, eggs, mashed plantain, melted butter (and vanilla/spice if using).
3. Add the **flour and salt** — start with ~450 g and add more only as needed. Keep the dough soft and slightly tacky; knead until smooth.
4. **First rise** — cover and rest until doubled (~1–1.5 hrs).
5. Shape into doughnuts and **proof** until puffy (~45–60 min).
6. **Fry** until deep golden on both sides, drain, then roll in sugar while still warm.

title: Three-Day Sourdough (soon…)
text: My next sourdough goal — the 3-day method I jotted down in class, the one I'm still working toward at the bench.

title: Three-Day Sourdough (soon…)
text:
> **An upcoming project.** This is the 3-day method I jotted down during my sourdough classes — my next goal at the bench. Until I get there, my everyday loaf is the lighter [Semi-Sourdough](/baking/semi-sourdough); photos will land here once I've baked a few of these.

The long one. Three days from a refreshed starter to a cold-proofed, Dutch-oven loaf — but most of that time the dough is just sitting in the fridge being patient. Here's the whole process the way I run it.

## The dough

A wholegrain-forward formula. Bread flour is ideally 12.2–13.8% protein.

- Bread flour — 560 g
- Whole wheat flour — 104 g
- Rye flour — 34 g
- Wheat germ — 52 g
- Water — 562 g
- Sea salt — 16 g
- Leaven — 100 g

## The soaker (night before)

Soak the wholegrains overnight in the chiller at **1–4 °C**:

- Whole wheat flour — 104 g
- Rye flour — 34 g
- Wheat germ — 52 g
- Water — 400 g

The remaining **balance water (162 g)** and **bread flour (560 g)** go in at **18–20 °C** the next morning.

## Building a starter from scratch

If you don't have a starter going, build one over ~14 days. Start with:

- Unbleached bread flour — 180 g
- Whole wheat flour — 20 g
- Honey (not raw) — 1 g

Then feed on a schedule (equal-ish flour & water, ~20–30 g each), keeping it at room temp (23–26 °C):

- **Day 1** — 30 g water + 30 g flour + 1 g honey
- **Day 3** — 30 g starter + 30 g water + 30 g flour
- **Day 5–6** — 20 g starter + 20 g water + 20 g flour
- **Day 10** — feed twice (9 am & 9 pm)
- **Day 11–14** — 20 g starter + 20 g water + 20 g flour

Keep a **backup starter** (20 g starter + 40 g flour + 40 g water); leave it ~2 hrs at room temp, then chill — good for up to 14 days.

## Building the leaven (day before)

Feed up to a mature leaven on bake-eve:

| Time | Starter | Water | Flour | Total |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1:30 pm | 10 g | 10 g | 10 g | 30 g |
| 6:30 pm | 10 g | 10 g | 10 g | 30 g |
| 11:30 pm | 15 g | 15 g | 15 g | 45 g |
| 4:30 am | 25 g | 50 g | 50 g | 125 g |

You need **100 g** for the dough; the leftover ~25 g goes back to daily starter maintenance. Final feed at **4:30 am** → matures by **~9:30 am** → use it between **9:30–10:30 am**.

## Bake day

1. **8:00 am** — mix the autolyse (balance water + bread flour + the soaker) and let it rest.
2. **~9:45 am** — mix in the leaven, folding slowly 4–5 min. Dough temp should be **under 25 °C**, ideally **22–23 °C**. If it's at 25 °C, cling-wrap and chill 10 min, hand-fold, chill 10 min more, until it's around **16–17 °C**.
3. Rest ~30 min at 22–23 °C.
4. Mix in the **sea salt** using the pinching method, 5–6 min on slow speed.
5. **Light fold** → 30 min → **lamination** (add any toppings here) → 30 min.
6. **Coil fold ×3**, 30–45 min apart.
7. **Bulk** a further 1–2 hrs.
8. **Divide / preshape** → 10–15 min bench rest → **shape** → 10 min.
9. Into the **proofing basket** → **cold proof overnight, 12–16 hrs at 1–3 °C**.

## The bake

Straight from the fridge into a screaming-hot **Dutch oven**:

- **20 min at 250 °C — lid on**
- **15 min at 200 °C — lid off**

Cool completely before cutting. (Cutting it warm is a beginner's mistake I still make.)

title: Why your crust goes hard (and how I fix it)
text: I kept getting complaints about leathery, knife-resistant crusts — mostly on low-yeast and sourdough loaves. These are the running notes — the factors I watch and the fixes that actually work.

title: Why your crust goes hard (and how I fix it)
text:
I kept getting complaints that the crust was too hard. So I started watching for the things that cause it. These are my running notes.

## First — what crust are you actually after?

Worth saying up front, because "softer" isn't a single goal. Steam plus a long, open bake gives you that crackly, rustic country-loaf crust — lovely, but the *opposite* of soft. A tender crust — sandwich loaves, milk bread, softer artisan loaves — is a different finish: less drying at the end, a bit more enrichment, and some help holding moisture in once it's out. So steam early for the spring, but don't expect the rustic-crust tricks to also hand you pillowy. They pull in opposite directions, and most of my "hard crust" fixes below are aimed at the soft end.

## Steam early, or the crust sets too soon

Low-yeast and sourdough breads need **steam at the start of the bake**. Without it:

- the crust sets too early,
- moisture escapes from the surface,
- and you get a thick, leathery, knife-resistant crust.

So I steam for the **first 10–15 minutes**.

## Watch the bake time

Low-yeast dough **browns slower but dries faster** if it's baked too long. Slow colour isn't a reason to leave it in longer — it'll dry out before it looks "done".

## Small things that hold the moisture in

- **Dust with rice flour, not wheat flour** (and lightly) — wheat flour pulls moisture off the surface; rice flour doesn't.
- **Increase the hydration** when you're working with low yeast.
- **Don't lean only on strong flour** — it can make the crumb chewy. Mix your flours up.

## Don't let it cool in dry air

The crust hardens if the loaf **cools in dry air**. So:

- cool it on a rack but **drape a cloth loosely** over it, or
- **brush the crust** right after it comes out:
  - **Water — but sparingly.** A very light mist or brush is plenty to soften it; any more and you're steaming it limp. This is the rye / sandwich / softer-European-loaf move.
  - **Butter — works a treat.** It's exactly why my sweet breads stay soft, so I go more generously with butter than I ever would with water.

## My Dutch oven routine

- Preheat the Dutch oven to **220 °C**.
- Bake **25 min, lid ON**.
- Bake **10–15 min, lid OFF** — watch the colour.

It's aggressive heat, so **lid on for longer and less time open** builds a softer, less firm crust.

---

These are all running notes, hacks and thoughts — still adding to them every bake.

title: Tiramisu — Old Monk instead of Marsala
text:
There's no single "classic" booze in tiramisu — the coffee is espresso, and the traditional spirit is Marsala, not a coffee brandy. I reached for Old Monk, and it more than holds its own.

I went looking for the "correct" tiramisu and found there isn't really one bottle. Two separate things are doing the work:

- **The coffee** is just strong **espresso** — that's what the ladyfingers get dipped in (straight from the moka pot, in my case).
- **The spirit** is a separate splash, and the *traditional* one is **Marsala**, a fortified Italian wine. Dark rum, brandy and coffee liqueur (Kahlúa) are all common stand-ins.

title: Tiramisu — Old Monk instead of Marsala
text:
I used **Old Monk**, and it more than holds its own — that vanilla-caramel warmth sits right alongside the espresso and the mascarpone. Not a compromise; if anything, I prefer it.

No baking here, technically — it's a fridge-set, no-bake assembly — but it came out of the same kitchen, and it came out *well*.

title: Kajjaya — the Deepawali sweet I grew up on
text:
A Kannada sweet my mum fries every Deepawali — crunchy outside the way she makes it, soft and gooey the way my grandpa liked it, with mashed banana. The (tricky) method is coming.

Kajjaya is a Karnataka sweet — dark, fried, a little sesame on top — and one of those smells I'll always file under Deepawali.

I like mine **crunchy on the outside**, which is how my mum makes them. Others keep them **soft, with a gooey centre**, and eat them with a bit of **mashed banana** — that's how my grandpa took his. He had a serious sweet tolerance, probably because sweets were rare back then; nothing like how often we reach for them now.

Mum makes a big batch every Deepawali. We share it around — neighbours, friends, relatives — and *still* have enough to snack on for the better part of a month.

title: Kajjaya — the Deepawali sweet I grew up on
text: The making is trickier than it looks. I'll write that part up properly soon.

title: Why autolyse is better in Bangalore (most of the time)
text: In a warm kitchen, autolyse buys you control over timing, stronger dough, and safer long rests. A note on why I lean autolyse here.

title: Why autolyse is better in Bangalore (most of the time)
text:
Bangalore's kitchen warmth changes the maths on autolyse vs fermentolyse. Here's why I mostly reach for a plain autolyse.

## 1. You control fermentation timing

- No starter in the mix → no fermentation yet.
- You decide exactly when bulk begins.
- Very useful in warm weather.

## 2. Stronger dough

- Gluten develops first, without acid interference.
- Gives better structure and oven spring.

## 3. Safer for longer rests

- You can autolyse 30–60 min (even longer) without risk.
- With fermentolyse, even 30 min can "eat into" bulk time.

**Especially useful if:**

- you're doing a high-hydration dough,
- you're using whole wheat / atta,
- your kitchen crosses 27 °C.

## The problem with fermentolyse in Bangalore

Because fermentation starts immediately, you lose control over timing — and the dough can get slack, overproof faster, and turn slightly sour earlier.

Bakers in warm kitchens say exactly this:

> "At ~29 °C things ferment too fast… autolyse helps develop gluten before fermentation starts."

## Bottom line

- **Hot kitchen (Bangalore)** → autolyse wins.
- **Cool kitchen / need speed** → fermentolyse.

title: Cricket
text: I bowl right-arm fast. I love fast bowling in all its forms. My bowling heroes are Dale Steyn, Glenn McGrath, Brett Lee. Favourite batsman: Sachin Tendulkar. IPL team: Royal Challengers Bengaluru (IPL).

title: Travel
text: I've travelled solo across 20 states and union territories of India: Karnataka, Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Puducherry, Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, Maharashtra, Goa, Gujarat, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Delhi, Punjab, Himachal Pradesh, Jammu & Kashmir, Ladakh, Bihar, West Bengal, Meghalaya, Assam.

title: LLM Flight-Search Pipeline
text: LLM Flight-Search Pipeline at Techtree Labs: A server-side flight-search pipeline using the OpenAI API with JSON-schema structured outputs, Zod validation, and anti-hallucination grounding against a live airport API. Stack: Next.js, Node.js, OpenAI API, Zod, PostgreSQL.

title: utravel.com
text: utravel.com (Techtree Labs, 2024 – present): Full-stack work across the travel platform — React/Next.js frontends and Node.js API routes — including the LLM flight-search pipeline featured above. Stack: Next.js, Node.js, OpenAI API, PostgreSQL.

title: Karnataka Govt · MGNREGA services
text: Karnataka Govt · MGNREGA services (Techtree Labs, 2024 – present): End-to-end React/Next.js + Node.js features for the state's MGNREGA rural-employment program — public-sector scale, real citizens, real accountability. Stack: Next.js, React, Node.js, PostgreSQL.

title: Thrillark

title: YouKraft marketplace

title: Nidhivana Farms & Resort
text:
Nidhivana Farms & Resort — in Mangaluru, Kerala. I rated it 5/5. Aesthetically build house in a beautiful farm. Located at the foothills of a small mountain with private access to the trekking trail, ideal right!
Well maintained rooms and lovely food (not to miss)
Overall a pleasant experience!.

title: Pasta Street - Cunningham Road
text: Pasta Street - Cunningham Road — in Bengaluru, Karnataka. I rated it 4/5. Sagar, was excellent at his serving and had all the knowledge to help us order the right things.

title: tribal brew daily | Indiranagar
text:
tribal brew daily | Indiranagar — in Bengaluru, Karnataka. I rated it 5/5. Ordering a cold brew and carrot cheese cake
Expecting some bold flavours

title: Running & riding
text: Across 132 logged activities I've covered about 777 km with 5663 m of climbing — 78 runs, 18 rides, 31 walks, 4 hikes. My longest run is 12.1 km.

title: Longest ride
text: Longest ride: "Ride to Gudibanda" — 137 km on 14 Jul 2023.

title: Longest walk
text: Longest walk: "Morning Walk" — 5.6 km on 15 Mar 2023.

title: Baking
text: Sourdough is mostly patience and a warm spot — I stopped fighting the clock and the loaves got better
